content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Underrated Comedy: Captain Ron (1992)

**Score:** 91
**Body:** Honestly I forgot all about this movie and then watched it recently and laughed my ass off. So many overlooked jokes and watching Martin Short play the straight man to Kurt Russell was brilliant.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “A diesel loves her oil same as a sailor loves rum”
  - "I learned that in rehab."
2. honestly, this is a bucket list movie for me

John Carpenter and Kurt laughing about this during the commentary for Big Trouble in Little China will always be a highlight 
3. "There's guerillas in these woods."
4. "He said gorillas not guerrillas. Ger- go- HUGE difference!"
5. I always like to pair Captain Ron with What About Bob? these two movies are like steak and wine.
6. Agree. Laughed my ass off.
7. It was one of those Kurt Russell films that I did not see until recently (along with Used Cars).   Both were very good movies that made me appreciate Kurt Russell even more.
8. I agree. Very fun!
9. A shark ate your eye?

## 2. Any movie like The Martian?

**Score:** 61
**Body:** It’s one of the most comforting movies for me. Something about it makes me watch it again and again. Maybe it’s Matt Damon doing random stuff alone.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moon

Sam Rockwell. He’s on the moon alone
2. Castaway?  Tom Hanks doing stuff alone to survive.
3. Project Hail Mary in theaters soon. Same author, similar concept but different enough.
4. Apollo 13
5. Not like Martian in the same sense, but Moon was a cool low budget sci-fi movie.
  - I'll watch moon, getting many suggestions
6. Competent people doing things competently.  

 \- Apollo 13 

 \- Sully

 \- Moneyball
7. Ad Astra

Gravity

Enemy Mine 

They're a bit darker though.
8. I liked Interstellar, he’s in that one too.
9. Try “All is Lost” with Robert Redford. He’s doing stuff alone.

## 3. Feel good movie to recommend?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Hello everyone,

Well, I've been a big fan of feel-good movies for a while now.

Here are a few that I really liked:

* Chef
* The Peanut Butter Falcon
* The Way Way Back
* Hunt for the Wilderpeople
* Born Again

Actually, what I'm looking for is that feeling of positive freedom, attitude, and wide open spaces.

Any recommendation ? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Little Miss Sunshine 2006

The Princess Bride 1987

The Intouchables 2011

Also, you might want to change your tag from 'I'm suggesting" to 'I'm Requesting' because you are asking for suggestions.
2. Lars and the Real Girl (2007)
3. Big Fish
  - Feel good move that will make you cry
4. Captain Fantastic
5. Babe ❤️
  - Username checks out lol! I loved Babe as a kid and when I saw James Cromwell play a super horrible person on American Horror Story I was devastated, but I guess that just goes to show what a good actor he is!
  - A little pig goes a long way. 😊
6. Happy-go-Lucky (2008) 

Amélie (2001)

The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013)

Little Miss Sunshine (2006)
  - Yeah, The secret Life of Walter Mitty is One or m’y favourite movie all time !

## 4. Name an English language film you love and I’ll recommend two that aren’t in English

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Thought this might be a fun thing to do.

Will try and match the vibe of the film you’ve chosen as closely as possible, and if there’s any part of the world you’d like to see more films from I can be specific too.

Just trying to share the love and suggest some great cinema from around the world! ❤️ 

Putting some more words here so that I don’t get caught out by the low effort filter 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Leon The Professional
  - The obvious answer would be another film by Luc Besson, like La Femme Nikita. Otherwise I’d say a John Woo film
2. 1. Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels (1998). 
2. The Birdcatcher (2019)
  - Man Bites Dog

Europa Europa
3. Alien!
  - Luc Besson’s debut The Last Battle has a similar kind of desolate science fiction vibe.
4. Blue Collar
  - I actually think No Other Choice could fit quite well in its use of a normal person turning to crime as a critique of capitalism

I’m also going to recommend L’Argent by Bresson as it’s such a beautiful film!
5. Monty Python and the Holy Grail
  - Donkey Skin by Jacques Demy

The Canterbury Tales by Pasolini

Impossible to match the same kind of British humour but these both have similar 1970s medieval vibes

## 5. Movies like Contagion

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Am searching for movies involving breakout virus or diseases but not necessarily zombie movies or movies like 28 days or world War z.

Anything you can recommend?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The 1993-1994 version of 'The Stand'.
2. Outbreak

Andromeda Strain
3. Andromeda Strain (1971)
4. 12 Monkeys is virus based.
5. Outbreak (1995) is a classic pandemic movie.
6. Stephen King's The Stand

12 Monkeys

Carriers
7. Weird how few there are, actually you would think there would be hundreds of written in 2020, esp indies.

The Masque of the Red Death (part of the Roger Cornan/Vincent Price “Poe cycle”)

Apocalypto (though the outbreak shown is anachronistic)  

Not a film but Deadwood has several episodes involving an epidemic
8. On the Beach
9. If you want to see something hilarious watch Pandemic from like 2007. Kelly from Saved by the Bell is a the CDC doctor, Eric Roberta* is the mayor of LA. Vincent Spano is playing a loose cannon FBI agent almost like he’s a vampire and French Stewart plays a straight guy lol. 

It’s really bonkers stuff
10. Perfect Sense (2011) is one of the most unique out there.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Recommendation

**Score:** 388
**Body:** Can you please, recommend movies that are like "Exit to Eden?" 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Do not ever rent it from blockbuster for family movie night thinking it’s a comedy because that’s how it was marketed and a follow-up that is related, don’t watch it with your parents. Speaking from experience.
  - Omg same thing happened to me, except my mom and I actually stuck it out incredulously to see how it ended. 

Badly. It ended badly. I cannot and will not help the OP find more movies like Exit to Eden.
    - It‘s based on an Anne Rice book. I remember reading it when I was in high school, back in the 80’s and 90’s. 
      - Book is awesome, movie is wild, but Dana Delaney.  Wow!!!!
  - At the movie theater, with my dad and uncle and two older cousins. We’re trying to decide what movie to see and none of us know anything about any of them, “hey Dan Aykroyd and Rosie O’Donnell are in this one.” I was 12 or 13 and that was awkward.
2. This flopped so hard Dana Delaney turned down Carrie Bradshaw in Sex and the City because she was wary of doing another project that was sexual.
  - Well, our loss
3. This book is based on a soft porn novel written by Anne Rice. It’s so hilarious to me that they made into a comedy.
4. My parents took me to see this. Mom thought a movie with Dan Ackroyd and Rosie O'Donnell would be wholesome. She spent the entire movie mortified she had taken her 12 year old son to this. Meanwhile, Dad kept saying "I spent good money, we're not leaving."
5. https://preview.redd.it/ogfso145qmmg1.jpeg?width=680&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3b197d8cda79d2fee29b38f6c77558e79eb8e68c

Rosie was a bit of alright.

## 2. It upsets me that there was never more to expand the lore of this movie

**Score:** 340

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sometimes just 1 film being really good is all that is needed. Don’t need to ruin it with sequels and prequels. Just one shot and hit the mark.
  - This goes underappreciated lately. Anything that ends is considered a failure.
    - "If some is good, then more is better!" And that's how we wind up with an obesity epidemic, ridiculously sized cars on the road, and people addicted to body mods where they get huge implants or inject oil into their biceps. And, of course, endless sequels/prequels/reboots.
2. It is a perfect sci-fi film imo.
Complete and monolithic.
3. that dark, post-apocalyptic world and the lore around the stitchpunk souls deserved way more exploration, shame it never got a sequel or series
4. It's pretty simple, it was a box office failure. That's the lore
  - This centuries, The Black Calduron, if you will.
    - Great comparison. It was too dark for kids and to kiddie for most adults.
5. Omg i loved this movie when i came out! i think i only watched it once!   
Thanks for reminding me i'm gonna watch it tonight again, all i remember is that elijah wood is voicing i think.
6. I mean considering it was based on a short film for a college assignment, I think they did well with the lore. They explained it enough to know what was going on and it ended with them winning. 

I do think this could have been a interesting series if expanded more and maybe not kill off some of the better ones right away, but its a kids movie that was a little too dark for kids imo which helped lead it to flop at release.

## 3. Which among these three is the best? Gravity, Interstellar or The Martian.

**Score:** 158
**Body:** Drop your opinions below. Do you think these films still hold up?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interstellar is gonna take it. But I fucking LOVE The Martian.
  - The Martian is one i rewatch occassionally, I can't say the same for interstellar honestly
  - The Martian is good, but having read the book before seeing the movie, I was so mad that they reduced his journey from the hab to the MAV to a montage.  The stuff he did to survive that journey was the most interesting and entertaining part of the book for me.  I was so disappointed it wasn't in the movie.  I understand why they did it for the movie (pacing would have been awful otherwise), but really pissed me off.
2. Interstellar is by far the best, then Martian and finally Gravity.
  - Bad Matt, Good Matt, no Matt
    - So "The Good, The Bad and the no Matt."?
3. Project Hail Mary loading up, it’s gonna be in this league
  - Project Hail Mary has elements of all 3 wrapped in one.
4. Interstellar is the one that feels more like an odyssé, martian is more light with loads of humor and gravity is gravity
5. Interstellar is in a whole other category apart from the others.

## 4. What’s the last good movie you watched?

**Score:** 142

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Really liked Bugonia. The acting was outstanding.
  - Plemons was outstanding. I love how his character slowly grew more and more out of control.
2. Rewatched *Galaxy Quest* last week
3. ![gif](giphy|WUs7UctXOuena3KP6E|downsized)
4. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
5. Watched Train Dreams last night... Breathtaking. 
6. I loved Send Help. My friend and I had such a fun time in the theater as did the rest of the audience. 
7. Train Dreams

I can't figure out why, in the discussions about good movies in 2025, this one isn't coming up more. It's one of the best movies I've seen in a long time. Maybe I'm just getting old, but the way it portrays this guy's totally ordinary life, with its highs and lows, as something beautiful floored me. Really helped me appreciate the beauty of my own totally ordinary life, with its highs and lows.
8. I watched The Menu this weekend, my wife and I absolutely loved it.
9. Rental Family

## 5. Harrison Ford's phenomenal speech at 32nd Annual Actor Awards... what a remarkable humble legend

**Score:** 126

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Very encouraging. I will follow the second half of this young thespian’s career with great interest.
  - ![gif](giphy|l3fZK7BgnNHSKpp4c)
2. If you’re not watching Shrinking on AppleTV, you should fix that. His speech in the season two finale really hits you in the feels.
3. Hot take: I'd say he's had a pretty good career.
4. Holy Shit. 


I wonder if he wrote that himself. It was a phenomenal speech. Not just the delivery (I don’t think there’s a living actor with a better resume than him), but the words were incredible and incredibly sincere. 


He’s just awesome.
  - He has mastered the art of adorable and wise but curmudgeonly snark in a lot of press so I was expecting that and was blown away by the sincerity. Choked me up a bit
5. Amazing human and great speech. Definitely something that needs to be resonated now and forever. Luck plays a big role in a lot of people’s lives. Right place and right time. 

Give it its due just as much as skill. 

6. Great speech. The fact that Woody and Ford are such tight friends is such a vibe and beyond cool. 🤩
7. Amazing speech.
8. Thanks for sharing his speech. It was amazing and  covered so well why great movies and TV shows move us emotionally. The most beautiful acceptance speech ever spoken. I enjoy all the various award shows and Harrison Ford 's speech goes down as the best.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. My thoughts on Marty Supreme after hearing Josh Safdie’s Rewatchables interview

**Score:** 114
**Body:** The discourse around Marty Supreme seems split. Many dismiss it as a series of "pointless scenes” while others are seeing it as a clever subversion of the sports genre. If you look at the film through the lens of Josh Safdie’s recent comments on The Rewatchables (specifically his obsession with cosmic intervention) a different story emerges.

Marty is a case study in narcissism. Milton Rockwell represents a dark trajectory for Marty, if not for “cosmic intervention” (Moses, the bath tub falling through the floor).

The film operates on the thesis of "Change”. It’s quite literally one of the first lines in the film (sampled from the Tears for Fears track). The humiliations Marty experiences aren't random vignettes. They’re the violent, necessary dismantling of a man who believes the universe was designed specifically for him.

What were your thoughts while watching Marty Supreme? I’m especially interested in hearing from the Safdie heads.

Also, not trying to spam but I put together a video discussing my thoughts here: https://youtu.be/Mo5oi-0V7IM?si=FkwFkg0G22a3Nbtx

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His mother lies she’s sick to get his attention.  
  
His love interest joins him in roleplaying and deception from the very first scene up until she also fools Marty himself into thinking she was beaten.  
  
Marty and Tyler, The Creator (sorry, I do not remember the character name) scam people for money.  
  
There are many other instances of this theme with other characters too.

To me, the supreme irony of Marty Supreme is that his world is full of play pretend people and yet the only moment in which he decides _not_ to take part on an arranged match, which _should_ be a redeeming moment (from a mere idealistic point of view) actually feels like a pyrrhic victory.  
  
This somehow subvert a sort of narrative baseline we often don’t really think about that character growth implies that the character’s world becomes a “better place” through such growth, which is not necessarily true. His growth feels necessary, but the means through which it finally happens is somewhat tragic considering the Japan match climax.
  - Yep he's a hustler, his mom's a hustler, and he dates a hustler just like his mom. People dating their parent
    - “She’s a grifter, just like her grifter brother. They probably had grifter parents and grifter grandparents and someday they’ll each spawn little grifter kids.”
  - I'm not sure I really agree with framing him refusing to throw the match as character growth. 

He never entertained throwing the match until it was serving the purpose of getting to the tournament after all other options were exhausted. As soon as he realized he wasn't in the tournament, he refused to throw the match.

There is quite literally no character growth demonstrated throught the whole movie aside from the implied potential for character growth of him accepting fatherhood at the end.
2. Marty Supreme is tonally uneven and a departure from the more consistent Good Time and Uncut Gems. 

I don’t think of it as a series of pointless scenes but it’s certainly episodic.

The acting by Chalamet is career best especially the climax in Japan and the final scene. Superb.

I have watched it twice now and I think both times the sub-plot with Paltrow just makes the film drag although it is a fine performance by her. 

I was stunned by the Milton Rockwell character on the first viewing but felt him less vital in the second viewing but I guess that is normal.

Marty Supreme is superbly cast. I enjoy the small roles that famous faces show up in and Abel Ferrara makes a great villain and he would probably be quite like some 1950s NYC gangsters.

The second watch was with a native New Yorker and she complained about some of the New York accents not being particularly authentic. Surely there are enough New Yorker actors available this shouldn’t be a problem?

I’m definitely interested in what Josh Safdie does next. I didn’t care for Benny’s The Smashing Machine. It just seemed to be the totally wrong choice of project if he’s going to make a UFC film. There would have been so many more intriguing stories to adapt and instead we got a domestic drama.

I know Marty Supreme is a crowd pleaser and many people enjoyed it. It didn’t crack my top 5 of the past year and I definitely prefer Uncut Gems and Good Time over Marty Supreme.
  - When I learned that that vampire line was an ad lib by Kevin O'Leary and that Josh Safdie thought that it was such a cool line that he *seriously* considered altering the entire ending of the movie, I realized Safdie never had a clear idea of what he wanted to say. 

There's such a huge difference between an ending with Marty tearfully looking at his new kid, and one where Marty ends up getting bitten by Milton Rockwell at an 80s rock concert.
    - Not sure that Marty getting bitten by Milton would have been a plot point but it’s an effective line for the antagonist to say he’s on top and he’s has been forever and he’s not going anywhere just like the billionaire class now.

I mean Josh Safdie becoming a father clearly has such a huge influence on the end of the film and it’s very personal. The ending is powerful but it’s not really consistent with the Marty character.
  - I think I liked Marty Supreme more than you but I basically agree with this. Good Time and Uncut Gems have lots of side plots and digressions, but they feel a bit more focused on the character getting what they want. Pattison in Good Time and Sandler in Gems have such laser focused wants and needs and are under such immense time pressure that the tension never really lets up, whereas “I need to raise money to get to Japan” is a bit looser, and so all those side quests and supporting characters feel a bit more shaggy dog and episodic. Even though the dog subplot provides a few of the film’s best moments, I felt like they also dragged a little and Marty really felt 2.5 hours, whereas Uncut Gems races by.
  - I didn’t find any scene in the movie to be tonally uneven. From a tone perspective, I’d argue that this movie was more meticulous, more cohesive than their previous two. 

Every character and every subplot felt intentional and necessary. And the movie didn’t drag for me at all. I wonder what tonal unevenness you’re referring to; I feel like every scene with Paltrow was critical to developing the story in a compelling way.
3. It's an interesting idea, but I think like any "cosmic intervention" explanation it raises more questions than it answers. Marty was warned about all of the things that go wrong (e.g. hotel clerk told him not to use the shower), how does that coincide with cosmic intervention? Why does the cosmic intervention start now? Why is it Marty who receives it, why doesn't Rockwell? 

Plus throughout the film it seems like Marty is the real source of most of the humiliations/problems he encounters.

## 2. Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner - the origins of the “White racist against the perfect Black man” trope in Hollywood?

**Score:** 71
**Body:** As someone from California, I used to joke that the OJ Simpson verdict was the “White liberal 9/11” because it exposed the “we are all the same” mentality that they been promoting for 25 years, especially in Hollywood. The 1990s was the “reality decade” in California, producing more cynically violent films about race relations, like Higher Learning, American History X, and Crash (all set in California btw). 

There’s another side to these films, ones that have more grounded, less openly hateful characters. From Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner to Driving Miss Daisy to the Green Book, but it becomes apparent that these films, made by White liberals, prefer to make the Black characters cartoonishly “perfect” and the White characters “tricked racists” who overcome their views by interacting with “one of the good ones”. At least the other ones I mentioned aren’t afraid to show White racists interacting with the “Black stereotypes” of criminals or nationalists, and still changing. 

In Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner case, the main White characters are liberal themselves and are essentially confronted with a NIMBY situation with a Black man, a perfect one, integrating into a “White living space” by marrying their White daughter. When push comes to shove, the liberal family shows their politics to be hypocritical and even elitist because they become divided on integration… when it affects them, “drain the pool” syndrome. 

The message is still respectable, but from a storytelling perspective, I think the film is quite poor because it’s essentially a metaphor for integration from a white centric perspective. Sidney Poitier is not a character, he’s just a “gotcha” symbol for Spencer Tracy. The interracial relationship is hardly depicted as a genuine relationship. It’s this one sided “Romeo and Juliet” story without attempting to develop the relationship romantically. The worst, it just reenforces African Americans need to be cartoonishly perfect to be accepted.

To word it differently, the minority characters are underdeveloped and one dimensional. A common trope that still exists in Hollywood. Even Spike Lee made himself a causal deadbeat in Do the Right Thing, not a Morgan Freeman. 

Who else sees this problem and how little Hollywood has changed over the decades?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think viewing the film as a failure of storytelling is a fair critique for the reasons you outline but it also largely ignores, beyond your note that the “message is still respectable,” the historical context that makes it a powerful piece of media. A question perhaps to follow up here would be “was storytelling the primary interest of this film or was it made to serve a different purpose?”

The film was released the year anti-miscegenation laws were rendered unconstitutional. It is likely that either script writing or filming or both took place in a time in which the interracial relationship would have been illegal in several states. I wonder if a more realistic depiction of the characters and their relationship could have been produced then. 

I think your question as to how little hollywood has changed since is well worth asking, i just think using this film as the jumping off point is a bit unfair.

  - > I wonder if a more realistic depiction of the characters and their relationship could have been produced then. 

And would such a depiction serve the aim of the story? If the goal was to make a clear statement in support of interracial relationships and calling out liberal hypocrisy, making their relationship messy and dramatic is going to work against that aim.
2. I broadly agree, but I think the violent films you contrast are really a different expression of the same phenomenon - i.e. stories about race made for white people that are really about how white people feel. In these stories it's the white characters' feelings and actions that are foregrounded, and the white characters who grow and learn while the black characters are deployed as secondary archetypes that serve as catalysts for white characters' growth. The difference is only really in the specific archetypes they employ - American History X deploys criminal/gang archetypes while Green Book deploys the "saintly victim of oppression" archetype, but they're both fundamentally the same story of a racist white guy learning to be less racist thanks to their interactions with black characters.

I think an illustrative contrast is between these films, which are about how white people experience their own racism, and something like Get Out, which despite having a mostly white cast is fundamentally about how the black protagonist experiences being in a white environment. In this film it's the white characters who are one-dimensional archetypes and whatever actions they take, the emphasis is always on how the black characters feel about it.
  - And just to clarify, there’s room for *all* of those stories.

Something like Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner may be simplistic in its depiction of a mixed-race relationship or of Poitier’s “perfect” character but those also simply aren’t what the film is exploring. It’s trying to rather flatly lay out the case that racism is still very present, even if more hidden, in liberal white America. Poitier’s character being nearly perfect is entirely intentional, as it shows that no matter what he’d accomplished or how polite he was, the parents still can’t see past the color of his skin. 

It’s a movie about racism made for white audiences, no doubt. But I think we can all agree that, for as long as we’ve had cinema, there have been plenty of white people who still need these messages and for their biases to be challenged.

The problem comes when the industry as a whole decides that it only wants to fund stories aimed at white audiences.
3. maybe this is 2026 of me, but I found that all four parents had a reasonable objection to the marriage nothing to do with race. Joanna is what 21? marrying a man almost twice her age who she has known for two weeks or something. maybe this was more common or acceptable 60 years ago. but for me the fundamental flaw of this movie is that the romance isn't believable nor substantiated. i'd rather it show a love story of equals where they have a strong foundation. as presented, the romance feels more like a temper tantrum than something worth fighting for. joanna and john might not make it but not because of race. but because they have nothing in common
  - This!  Maybe it's my own age, but the age difference and the fact that John had already gone through one divorce, would definitely IMO have made his parents wayyyyyyyy more resistant to him marrying her than the other way around.

It's another example of black families being platformed as "props" in narratives about how white people handle being culturally raised in white racism.

I like the remake for the way it flips the script.  It's also this idea that wealthy black families "deep inside" are pining to be part of the white hoi polloi because it's the elite.   No it isn't.   I baffles me, how many white women I know, who honestly believe that their black friends really wish they looked WHITE or were born white.   Um.  NOPE.  (excuse the pun)

It also amazes me how many white middle class people do not have a way to think of black families beyond "ghetto" or "bougie" or "mega rich sports stars and rappers".   The idea that many black families are just successful intact upper middle class families , is sort of a blind spot for them.
4. Dr. John Wade Prentice, played by Sidney Poitier in Guess Who's Coming to Dinner, is almost flawless and borders on the ridiculous. 

He is a highly educated, distinguished African American physician and expert in tropical medicine. He works for the World Health Organisation and occasionally travels to Africa.

He is exemplary, honorable and intelligent. The audience and the other characters have sympathy for him as is also a widow.

Hollywood really had to make Poitter’s character like this to make more people comfortable about family members having an inter-racial relationship or miscegenate.

When I watched this film we laughed about this.
5. Calling John perfect is understandable, but why should he or Joanna should be imperfect? I think the flaws are left to the families, because the couple represents love and love itself is flawless in concept, so we shouldn't be looking for reasons to separate these two as an audience and instead look for the flaws in the people who are up against it.

Saying "it just reenforces African Americans need to be cartoonishly perfect to be accepted." seems out of place here, because he's rejected *despite* being perfect, and I would add that John's father is just as problematic as Joanna's father, and they both mask their true feelings of intolerance through projecting it onto society, it's never them of course, it's everyone else who has the problem. This feels like a very evenhanded approach imo where neither family is clearly above the other.
  - Yes, it isn't that the Black character has to be perfect, it's that even if he's perfect (or "perfect" by their measure, I should clarify), even if you remove every other excuse white racists might make against integration, they will still find a way to reject him.
    - That perfection may have been needed to make a point with white liberal audiences.  I grew up in a somewhat backwards area, and any normal human deficiencies would be attributed to race for something a white Christian male would get a pass on.  If a black character was struggling to find work in a bad economy after getting a Ph. D., they would be dismissed at stupid or lazy or have their degree attributed to white guilt.

## 3. I just saw Fish Tank (2009) for the second time

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I'm a 34 year old guy. I first watched this maybe at age 22 after seeing Andre Arnold's short film Wasp and really liking that, and liking this. But it sort of faded from memory since. Since then I've seen American Honey a few times, which I've always loved. I decided it was time to come back to this. Wow this movie is fantastic, and a lot. I definitely forgot the ending so watching it this time was intense.

There's a lot to this movie. It feels to me a lot about a character learning they're in a world they don't have control over. I also am a writer and there's some things about the form of this movie I find really interesting. I thought I knew every major story structure but this reminds me there's another form of story. Basically a character goes on a fucked up adventure, barely survives. The learning point is that they realize they're in over their heads, or that they've gone down a path they can't sustain. And they end the movie by entering some realm of normalcy voluntarily, be that rehab, or in this movie's case, >!boarding school!<.

Other thoughts about this:

-The cinematography was absolutely fantastic. 

-And two, Andrea Arnold is one of the only directors I think can pull off the "lower class" style of movie. I feel like there's such authenticity in every movie she makes. In my opinion I think she's way better than Sean Baker. When I watch his movies, while entertained, there's this feeling inside of me that says something isn't right, or authentic, or fair, or something. When I watch an Andrea Arnold movie, particularly this one, it feels like she's really seeing this character and situation and story. I leave it feeling sad but that this person is seen. Same with her mom. And sister. I feel we really understand them at the end. Wow. What a movie.

-I read an interview with Andrea Arnold after https://www.hammertonail.com/interviews/a-conversation-with-andrea-arnold-fish-tank/ She does some really unique stuff. She does not let the actors know how the movie is going to go, and they only see the next day's script the night before filming. Because she believes it will change how they play the early scenes if they know what the end scenes will be. I think that is a wild creative choice, and in watching the movie I can really feel that. I think you can tell the actors don't know what's coming, and I think that makes it better. I'm an emerging filmmaker myself, I don't know if I would ever do that. Maybe largely out of fear. And yeah she didn't allow tripods and everything is basically handheld. The way she gets so much characterization out of just following a character is really next level, and also makes the movie really breathe and not feel stuffy like most movies. The only other movie I've seen do something similar is maybe The Wrestler.

Anyways, this is one of my all time favorite movies now I think. If you haven't seen it, there's some real content warnings here so watch at your own risk. Sort of *Kids* adjacent.

## 4. PT Anderson and the elusive auteur

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I just rewatched Boogie Nights last night, and was again blown away by its sweeping scope and multiple impeccably-crafted (if a bit showy) long takes. But it made me reflect that PT Anderson, a guy who's been making films for decades now and has possibly the most iconic filmography of the 21st century, is a bit of an enigma to me. I look through his filmography and have many favorites (Boogie Nights of course, but also Punch Drunk Love and The Master, while recognizing the technical genius behind TWBB and Phantom Thread) but at the same time, I'm struggling to see an artistic through-line going across his acclaimed career. Like, if I watch a Sean Baker film or a Yorgos Lanthimos film, or a Scorsese film...it feels like each of these is so infused with the persona of its director, that it couldn't be made by anyone else, and I feel this kinship with their world view. PT Anderson films though, which very technically distinct (you could never mistake some of intricately-planned shots with anyone else), doesn't feel like he has this same level of personal imprint.

Am I crazy feeling this way? Is there a signature worldview that you get from PT Anderson that you would summarize and apply to the sweep of his career? Or does he not have it and it doesn't really matter because he's such a damned great technical filmmaker? Or are there other directors you feel this about instead...directors you admire but maybe can't quite find the connection to love?

(Also note: having not seen One Battle After Another yet, my opinions may be a bit incomplete, but this is something that's been at the back of my mind for years now)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His narrative attention is generally on close interpersonal or familial relationships, with the caveat that these are often in some way unconventional or even toxic. His entire corpus more or less is focused exclusively on this particular part of human experience. He rarely does much else.

Boogie Nights is a movie very much about how the characters lose their place in their society and/or birth family and establish a new family with each other.  Obviously it has a much more optimistic tone and resolution than some of his other work.

To me, he kind of tells you what he's interested in via Magnolia, which is very earnest, and he's been making movies with some variation of that lens ever since.

The poignant moments between characters are his thing. Even Inherent Vice, which is quite ironic and detached has these really affecting Shasta/Doc and Bigfoot/Doc moments as the plot resolves.
  - If I could add one thing, I'd point to specifically father-son relationships, whether literal or surrogate, as a major thread running throughout most of his filmography.
  - In the Inherent Vice novel, the "brotherhood" between Doc and Bigfoot is a huge underlying theme throughout the story. I loved how PTA did their last on-screen interation. 

Incidentally, >!Bigfoot, feeding Doc info on his case and genuinely helping him, but still manipulating Doc to take out Adrian Prussia!< is exactly the type of intimate/toxic relationship that PTA excels at.
2. Oh there is one. Misfits and lost souls seeking community/belonging/family. Boogie Nights is the prime example, it's not a movie about porn. It's a movie about a bunch of lost/flawed people looking for family. Magnolia is a bout a bunched of flawed people looking for connection. Punch Drunk Love, a misfit looking for connection. Hard Eight even has some of that with John C Reilly.

The main exceptions tend to be PTA movies based on existing material, so TWBB and Inherent Vice aren't really about this, but I'd argue the elements are mildly there.

The Master is another prime example, and probably my favorite example. A lost soul DESPERATELY seeking connection and finding it. Licorice Pizza, another set of misfits that connect. 

To be quite honest, I didn't totally get PTA until I made this realization.
  - And of course DDL willing to drink poison soup because of how desperate he is for connection in Phantom Thread.
    - That psycho was probably poisoning himself to prep for the movie.
3. There's one obvious artistic throughline: Los Angeles is the setting of six of PTAs ten features; three are set in PTA's childhood San Fernando valley.

In this, PTA has more of a focus on a place than Scorsese has to New York City, or to crime dramas. Though Sean Baker's thematic consistency (sex work features in the last 5 of 7 features) is higher than either.
  - I think this is a good point.

I'd also add that all of his films, even those not set in Southern California, have a very strong sense of time and place. 
4. Maybe not so much thematically, but I think there's a notable stylistic trait to mention, which is that he is in that rare club of directors who have a **signature shot**.

Ozu has the Tatami shot. Paul Thomas Anderson's inspiration and mentor Robert Altman had the slow zoom into a character's face.

[PTA, especially early on](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ihp4T6PLlXE), has the whip pan as a stylistic signature. 


5. You’re not crazy. I think the reason PTA can feel hard to pin down is that his movies do not clearly announce a moral stance the way Scorsese or Lanthimos do.

But there is a through-line. He keeps coming back to broken and surrogate families: porn crews, oil dynasties, cults, fashion houses. They are all small worlds built around one intense, charismatic guy, and the movie watches how that dynamic plays out: power, control, ego, emotional neediness. Those are the real subject.

What changes are his styles. The early movies like *Boogie Nights* and *Magnolia* are loud and emotional, full of speeches and big ensemble energy. The later ones like *There Will Be Blood*, *The Master*, and *Phantom Thread* are more stripped down and internal. There is less explaining and more silence. You have to read faces, framing, and body language. That shift can make it feel like there is no consistent worldview, when really the focus just became more concentrated.

He is also obsessed with performance, especially male performance. His main characters are always trying to force themselves into greatness or dominance. And PTA does not clearly tell you whether to admire them or judge them. He lets them exist in all their intensity.

So the signature might not be a clear philosophy. It is this ongoing focus on American ambition, masculinity, and fragile intimacy, explored in different tones each time.

## 5. American Beauty: Vulnerability, Ordinariness, and the Female Persona

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I just watched American Beauty and what a wonderful movie. So deep. I love movies that are deep and sensual and make me feel something, and this movie made me feel something. I feel like I see myself in all the characters, but especially the ordinary girl. She’s so afraid to be boring. And I think most women feel that, like the biggest insult is being called boring or ordinary. But when you really think about it, there’s nothing wrong with being ordinary or boring. I actually think boring is the best thing ever. There’s nothing wrong with it. I think the quiet people are usually the sensual ones, not the loud confident ones society points to. People think sensual means loud, sexy, performing confidence, like Angela in American Beauty or Maddy in Euphoria, but those characters are insecure and performing. The real sensual ones are the quiet ones, like Jane. You feel it from them. People don’t understand what sensual really is.

I feel like everyone needs to be boring because everyone is performing all the time. People don’t mind their business because they don’t want to be ordinary. They don’t want to be boring. People are always talking about others, gossiping, staying busy, distracting themselves, not sitting still and thinking about themselves. There’s nothing wrong with being boring and quiet and thinking. But when I was young people saw me as weird for being quiet. There was nothing wrong with that. I just didn’t express feelings the way people expected. I’m blunt and direct and people think I’m mean or off. Sometimes I wonder if something about my personality just doesn’t land right. I spend so much time thinking, so when I talk I talk about myself, because that’s what I’m actually thinking about. I don’t care about celebrities or what people are doing. I care about my own actions, mistakes, feelings. I think about myself. I observe people, yes, but in my head I’m processing myself. I don’t see why that’s wrong. What’s wrong with being quiet and thinking more. That’s how the world should be. There’s nothing wrong with that.

With Cassie from Euphoria, when that guy called her boring, it shouldn’t have destroyed her. She could have accepted it and realized yes I’m ordinary and that’s fine. She keeps performing and humiliating herself because she refuses ordinariness. There’s nothing wrong with being boring. People hear boring as worthless but I don’t mean that. I mean normal, human, not performing. Cassie and Maddy are actually the same. Both define themselves through desirability and validation. Cassie just does too much so society notices and mocks her, while Maddy is controlled so society praises her. But both are performing. Both are insecure. Both are hurt. I love deep movies because they show this inner truth.

I hate how society treats Cassie and Carrie Bradshaw harshly but worships Maddy and Samantha Jones. It’s the same pattern. The vulnerable, needy women get mocked, the confident armored ones get called queens. But they’re all flawed, hurt, imperfect people. Cassie, Carrie, Maddy, Samantha, they’re the same type underneath. They all need love and validation. They’re all wounded. They all need therapy honestly. I think I’m drawn more to Cassie and Carrie because they’re more visibly vulnerable. They’re exposed. They’re not hiding. Maddy and Samantha feel fake to me, like they act above everything. Maybe I’m projecting, but I see sweetness and hurt in Cassie and Carrie.

Social media now feels weird. Nobody thinks. People flatten everything into strong versus weak. They hate Cassie and Carrie and praise Maddy and Samantha without seeing they’re the same wounds. Social media is full of gender hostility too, people projecting hurt from relationships. You can’t even say you like men without being judged. But hurt people still want love. Humans are humans. Men and women can both be toxic, insecure, cheating, needy, perform confident when they’re not. Women aren’t saints. Both genders can act the same ways. These are human flaws, not gendered.

Honestly sometimes I feel like people should just go handle their relationships and intimacy in real life and leave social media lighter and fun. There’s so much bitterness online. I just want spaces that feel human and thoughtful.

Marilyn Monroe fits all of this too. She was sensual and vulnerable and judged. People even body shamed her even though she was beautiful, because culture both desires and fears visible femininity and softness. She’s another example of sensuality plus vulnerability being misunderstood and attacked.

I think eventually people online will start understanding Cassie and Carrie more. Culture moves in cycles. People will see the hurt under the behavior. They’re not pathetic, they’re human. And there is nothing wrong with being ordinary or boring. Boring just means you’re not performing. And that’s actually the most real state a person can be.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I still think American Beauty holds moments of great power, although Spacey's legacy somewhat taints Lester, and some of the stuff with Ricky and Jane doesn't age well for me -- I don't think we needed onscreen nudity from Birch (still a legal child), and Ricky's final "look of wonder" at Lester is utterly ridiculous and breaks immersion for me. Nobody reacts like that to the sudden horrific shock of death and violence in a friend -- not unless they're an overwritten drama kid cliche in a movie, that is. (I give Bentley credit for making Ricky as complex and likable as he did despite that.)

However, I agree that there shouldn't be shame attached to being ordinary. 

Yet I wouldn't say that word applied to Angela or really any character you mention here-- she was visibly beautiful, smart, and ambitious. She wasn't ordinary at all, but she still feared being described that way, so she lied, pretended a sexual promiscuity she didn't have, and puffed herself up to seem important. Not unusual for a teenager and certainly forgivable. And I definitely can't agree with you that Angela was not every bit as vulnerable as Jane, in her own way.

Jane is slightly easier to like because we see her struggles and insecurities more clearly and much earlier on. She's the quiet loner we're meant to empathize with, like Ricky. 

The real trick for me is how the movie presents Angela as some kind of knowing, cruel teenaged temptress then pulls the rug out from under us to show this as a lie. She's just an insecure virginal young girl already constantly dealing with men and boys sexualizing her. So she copes by sexualizing herself first, and by toying with Lester until things get too real -- and thankfully he comes to his senses and sees her as the child she actually is.

Which is why I am not going to delve into an analysis of either girl's "sensuality" since that honestly doesn't feel appropriate or very ultimately relevant. 

To me, American Beauty is ultimately about the truths beneath the lies, the rot beneath the rose, the dangers of image over substance. And as Lester slyly reminds us, we may buy into it, but we all see the truth eventually... or we will.

As far as themes go, it's worth noting that the original format of the film was far darker and more cynical. It was originally shot and framed as a crime noir with bookended courtroom scenes where Jane and Ricky stood trial for -- and were convicted of -- Lester's murder. But Sam Mendes and the editors ended up cutting 17 plus pages of action for the final film. (It's also why I think Alan Ball didn't deserve the screenwriting Oscar he won -- despite its wonderful dialogue, the film had to be massively re-edited to work at all.)

Meanwhile, I disagree with you on Carrie from SATC -- I didn't loathe her because she was vulnerable, I hated her because she was repeatedly, mind-bogglingly selfish, thoughtless, and cruel. And because for a sex writer she was surprisingly unimaginative and prudish.

And I loved Samantha because while she could be brash, crass, or occasionally hypersexual or oversexed (although that's debatable), she was also warm, honest, loyal, brave, imaginative, and kind. And unlike Carrie, she was rarely a hypocrite.

Angela and Samantha are not worse people because they're outgoing and sexy. And Jane and Carrie are not better people because they are more obviously vulnerable, although I struggle with your analogy since I don't think Carrie is all that similar to Jane or that she hides her light in any ways that Jane does.

I do agree that society tends to overlook the beauty and sexual potential or charisma of quieter women, especially in film and TV.
2. I’m sorry, but my attention in American Beauty was on the guy filming a plastic bag flowing in the wind. He was insecure and performing the whole time, too, but that’s the fun of it. And speaking of the character you liked: Jane presented herself as anything but ordinary, that kind of edgy teenage girl, which was also a performance. But yeah, why not? Maybe that’s the question you could ask yourself before sorting them into categories.
